gate_type: wp_nonce admin-only in default config; capability check via filterable $this->capability
reasoning: The exploitation chain has two gates: (1) current_user_can($this->capability) at line 589 of ajax_process_image(), and (2) wp_verify_nonce($frt_wpnonce, 'force-regenerate-attachment') at line 592. In the default configuration, $this->capability = apply_filters('regenerate_thumbs_cap', 'manage_options') resolves to 'manage_options'. The nonce 'force-regenerate-attachment' is generated at line 192 inside admin_enqueues() via wp_create_nonce(), which only executes when the plugin's management page is loaded ($hook_suffix === $this->menu_id). That admin page is registered at line 91 with the same $this->capability as the required capability, so it is only accessible to users with 'manage_options' (i.e., administrators). A subscriber or unauthenticated attacker cannot load the admin page in a default install, and therefore cannot obtain the nonce embedded via wp_localize_script(). Without the nonce, the AJAX handler at wp_ajax_regeneratethumbnail rejects the request before reaching unlink(). The vulnerability is CONDITIONAL: if a co-installed plugin hooks 'regenerate_thumbs_cap' and returns a lower capability (e.g. 'read'), a subscriber can (a) access the admin page because both the page registration and the AJAX capability check use the same lowered capability, (b) retrieve the nonce from the page source, and (c) invoke ajax_process_image() with a valid nonce, reaching unlink() on thumbnail files. This co-installed-plugin scenario is real but non-default and requires a second vulnerable or malicious plugin. In a default WordPress install with no other plugins lowering the filter, the gate is not breachable by low-privilege or unauthenticated users. Severity is downgraded from MEDIUM to LOW for default-install assessment; the conditional (co-plugin) scenario retains MEDIUM severity.
